title: Racial Primacy in the Eye of the Beholder: White Respondents’ Normative Judgments of Mixed- and Same-Race Couples
abstract: Existing research shows that some interracial relationship configurations, such as White and East Asian, particularly White men with East Asian women, are far more common than others, such as Black and East Asian. This raises the question: how do Americans evaluate different types of same- and mixed-race couples? After all, third-party evaluations shape which unions are socially encouraged or discouraged and reveal broader norms about intergroup boundaries and relationship legitimacy. The author examines how White Americans evaluate various same- and mixed-race pairings, such as White and Black versus White and East Asian couples, and White men with Black women versus White women with Black men. Using a novel dyadic conjoint experiment, a national sample of White respondents evaluated fictional dating app couples, making it possible to causally identify how race and gender shape third-party perceptions. The results show a consistent preference for same-race couples: respondents rated them more favorably, believed others would do so too, and saw them as more compatible and enduring. Contrary to theories of racialized sexuality, partner gender played a limited role in evaluations of interracial couples. The key exception was among White men, who disapproved more of mixed-race couples involving White women. These findings advance research on racial homophily, racialized sexuality, and third-party perceptions, revealing how racial boundaries shape imagined futures and relationship legitimacy.
